headline: Volkswagen Is Betting Big on India Again—Here's Why
summary: Volkswagen is preparing one of its most ambitious India offensives in years, committing to product interventions every quarter, expanding its premium portfolio, and evaluating future opportunities across hatchbacks, SUVs, exports, and electrification. The strategy signals a long-term commitment to India rather than a short-term sales push, with significant implications for buyers, competitors, and the wider automotive market.
seo_title: Volkswagen Is Betting Big on India Again in 2026
meta_description: Volkswagen is expanding its India strategy with new launches, premium SUVs, future EV plans, and a renewed focus on growth.

article_html:
Volkswagen Is Betting Big on India Again—Here's Why

Summary: Volkswagen is preparing a broad-based expansion strategy for India that extends far beyond a single vehicle launch. With quarterly product interventions, a stronger premium positioning strategy, future electrification plans, export growth ambitions and a willingness to explore new segments, the company appears determined to strengthen its relevance in one of the world's most important automotive markets.

Introduction

For much of the past decade, Volkswagen's position in India has been defined by selective success rather than outright dominance. The company built a loyal enthusiast following, earned a reputation for solid engineering, and introduced several well-regarded products. Yet it often struggled to match the scale and reach achieved by the market's largest players.

That appears to be changing. Recent announcements and executive commentary reveal a company that is once again investing significant attention, resources and planning into India. The latest roadmap includes multiple product interventions across body styles, a stronger focus on premium positioning, export expansion, future technology adoption and continued development of its India-focused portfolio.

Viewed together, these actions suggest something larger than a routine product cycle. They point to a renewed long-term commitment to India.

What Happened

The most visible part of the strategy is an unusually active product calendar. Volkswagen has committed to introducing new product actions throughout the year, ensuring customers see fresh developments on a regular basis rather than waiting years between major announcements.

The flagship Tayron R-Line represents one of the most significant pieces of that plan. Positioned as a premium offering, it strengthens Volkswagen's aspirational image while providing a halo effect for the broader lineup.

Alongside flagship products, Volkswagen continues to rely heavily on its locally developed India-focused vehicles. The Taigun remains particularly important, serving as one of the brand's primary volume drivers. Company leadership has indicated expectations for stronger sales performance following recent updates, highlighting the vehicle's strategic role.

At the same time, the company is evaluating future opportunities in additional segments, including hatchbacks and potentially other high-volume categories.

The Strategy Behind the Push

Three themes emerge clearly from Volkswagen's current direction.

Premium Positioning

Unlike several rivals pursuing aggressive volume growth through every available segment, Volkswagen continues to emphasize premium appeal. Performance-oriented variants have reportedly become an increasingly important part of sales, suggesting buyers are responding positively to products positioned above the mainstream.

This strategy allows the company to differentiate itself rather than competing solely on discounts or fleet volume.

Consistent Product Activity

One challenge many global brands face in India is maintaining momentum between launches. Volkswagen's commitment to regular product interventions addresses that issue directly.

Frequent activity keeps the brand visible, helps dealerships maintain customer interest and gives potential buyers more reasons to revisit showrooms.

Long-Term Market Building

The broader strategy goes beyond product launches. Export growth, customer experience initiatives, network development and future technology planning all indicate a company attempting to strengthen its foundation for sustained growth rather than chasing short-term headlines.

Missing Details and Open Questions

Despite the ambitious roadmap, several important questions remain unanswered.

First, Volkswagen has not fully detailed the complete product lineup planned under its quarterly intervention strategy. Buyers know activity is coming, but many specifics remain undisclosed.

Second, electrification plans remain directional rather than definitive. The company acknowledges EVs are part of its future, yet timelines, localisation strategies and model choices have not been finalized publicly.

Third, discussion around hatchbacks has generated considerable interest. Enthusiasts continue to associate the brand with successful premium hatchback products, but there has been no official confirmation regarding a direct revival of any specific model.

Fourth, the company's position on hybrid technology remains cautious. While management recognizes transitional technologies may play a role, the exact mix of powertrains remains unclear.

Finally, any future entry into highly competitive volume segments would require careful balancing between brand identity and scale ambitions.

Contradictions and Industry Challenges

One of the most interesting aspects of Volkswagen's strategy is that it appears to challenge several prevailing industry assumptions.

Many manufacturers have concentrated almost exclusively on SUVs as hatchback demand declines. Volkswagen, however, continues to discuss opportunities in hatchbacks alongside SUVs.

There is also an apparent tension between premium positioning and the pursuit of higher volumes. Premium strategies can support profitability and brand image, but India's largest growth segments are often intensely price-sensitive.

Similarly, Volkswagen believes performance remains a major selling point for its customers. Yet broader market trends increasingly prioritize fuel efficiency, hybrid technology and ownership costs.

These are not necessarily contradictions in the negative sense. Instead, they highlight the challenge of balancing brand identity with evolving consumer preferences.

What It Means for Buyers

For consumers, Volkswagen's renewed focus on India could create several benefits.

More frequent product updates typically result in fresher vehicles, improved technology and stronger feature competitiveness.

Premium buyers may also benefit from broader choices. The strategy indicates continued investment in aspirational products rather than limiting the lineup to mainstream offerings.

Potential EV buyers should watch developments closely. While immediate announcements remain limited, Volkswagen's global expertise in electrification means future introductions could become significant when market conditions align.

Existing owners may benefit from network improvements and stronger long-term brand commitment. Confidence in future support often influences purchase decisions as much as product specifications.

Buyers should nevertheless remain realistic. Not every future possibility discussed by management will necessarily become a production reality. Product plans can evolve in response to market conditions, regulations and business considerations.

Market Impact

Volkswagen's strategy arrives at a pivotal moment for India's passenger vehicle industry.

SUVs continue to dominate growth, competition is intensifying across price points and electrification remains an evolving opportunity rather than a settled outcome.

By maintaining interest in multiple body styles, Volkswagen is effectively arguing that the market remains more diverse than headline sales figures suggest. If successful, the strategy could encourage greater investment in niche premium segments that many competitors have deprioritized.

The export component is equally important. Strong export programs improve manufacturing utilization, support localisation and strengthen India's position within global automotive supply chains.

From an industry perspective, Volkswagen's actions also reinforce confidence in India's long-term potential. Global manufacturers do not commit resources to multi-year expansion plans unless they see meaningful opportunities ahead.

Competitor Impact

The company's renewed ambition is unlikely to go unnoticed.

Established SUV leaders may face additional pressure as Volkswagen expands its premium offerings and strengthens showroom traffic.

Brands competing in performance-oriented segments could encounter more direct rivalry if Volkswagen continues emphasizing enthusiast-focused products.

Manufacturers balancing hybrids, EVs and conventional powertrains will also watch closely. Volkswagen's eventual technology choices could influence broader industry discussions about the most effective transition pathways for India.

Perhaps most importantly, rivals may need to respond to the consistency of Volkswagen's product cadence. Regular launches help maintain relevance and media visibility throughout the year.

Future Outlook

Looking ahead, several developments deserve attention.

The success of upcoming product interventions will provide an early indication of whether Volkswagen's strategy is resonating with customers.

Progress on trade agreements could potentially influence the speed with which global products reach India.

Electrification plans will remain a major area of interest, particularly as infrastructure, regulations and consumer acceptance continue to evolve.

Any movement toward premium hatchbacks or new SUV segments would also attract significant market attention.

Ultimately, the company's long-term success will depend on execution rather than announcements. Product quality, pricing, ownership experience and network strength will determine whether the renewed push translates into sustained growth.

Conclusion

Volkswagen's latest moves suggest a company preparing for a larger role in India's automotive future. The strategy combines premium positioning, consistent product activity, export growth, future technology planning and selective market expansion.

Not every question has been answered, and several important decisions remain ahead. Yet the broader direction is increasingly clear. Rather than treating India as a secondary market, Volkswagen appears to be positioning it as a significant part of its global growth story.

For buyers, competitors and industry observers alike, that makes the next few years worth watching closely.

FAQ

Why is Volkswagen increasing its focus on India?

India remains one of the world's fastest-growing automotive markets and offers opportunities across premium vehicles, exports and future electrification.

What products are part of Volkswagen's current strategy?

The company has outlined multiple product interventions across SUVs, sedans and hatchbacks, supported by flagship premium offerings and updated volume models.

Is Volkswagen planning EVs for India?

The company has indicated electrification is part of its long-term strategy, although specific timelines and models have not been fully confirmed.

Could premium hatchbacks return?

Volkswagen has acknowledged continued interest in hatchbacks, but no definitive return of a specific model has been officially announced.

What does the strategy mean for buyers?

Buyers can expect more product activity, broader choices, improved engagement and potentially stronger long-term investment in the Indian market.

research.title: An exciting year ahead: Volkswagen India plans 5 product interventions for 2026
research.description: An exciting year ahead: Volkswagen India plans 5 product interventions for 2026
research.text_excerpt: Volkswagen is proud to announce its exciting plans for 2026 with new product interventions in every quarter of the year Product actions will include SUV, Sedan and Hatchback body styles Tayron R-Line marks the first product intervention, 4 more actions to follow through the year for an action packed and definitive brand calendar Mumbai : Volkswagen India today showcased its first product for 2026 – Tayron R Line. The brand also announced its plans for product interventions in every quarter of the year, aimed at preserving relevance and market excitement through the calendar year 2026. The 5 product interventions planned in 2026 will be based on SUV, Sedan and Hatchback body styles, each catering to a different premium customer audience. For 2026, the brand has outlined its macro-objectives that are defined by Aspirational Premium products, enhanced Customer Engagement and exclusive Experiences. Speaking on the occasion, Mr. Nitin Kohli, Brand Director, Volkswagen Passenger Cars India , said, “Today, we are glad to showcase the Tayron R-Line for the first time in India. I am also delighted to announce that we have planned four more product interventions throughout the year. This year, every quarter will witness a new product intervention that will cater to a different premium customer set. Our objective is to continue building excitement for customers through smart product actions and introducing models that will continue to build aspirations.”

research.title: Nitin Kohli on Volkswagen’s India strategy, new launches and Tayron R-Line plans | ET Auto
research.description: Volkswagen India plans to strengthen its position in the premium segment in 2026, guided by a strategy built on three pillars: aspiration, customer experience..
research.text_excerpt: Volkswagen India’s 2026 strategy will focus on strengthening its premium positioning while balancing aspiration-led products with volume models, Brand Director Nitin Kohli told ETAuto. The company plans at least one model intervention every quarter, alongside the launch of the flagship Tayron R-Line, targeting around 1,600–1,800 units this year. Volkswagen’s strategy rests on three pillars—aspiration, customer experience and engagement—while its premium GT variants now account for nearly 60% of Tigun and Virtus sales. Kohli also highlighted stable dealer expansion, optimism around market growth, and the potential impact of free trade agreements on faster technology access. research.title: Inside Volkswagen India’s 2026 Strategy: Hatchback, Taigun Growth & Export Plans
research.description: India’s passenger vehicle market is going through one of its fastest structural shifts in decades. According to industry data by Vahan Bazaar, SUVs now command 58 per cent of all cars sold in FY2026, up sharply from 52 per cent in FY25 and from just 37 per cent three years ago, highlighting how SUVs have become the primary growth engine for the PV industry. At the same time, a segment that once defined India’s car-buying culture ‘hatchback’ is shrinking. Hatchbacks have declined by 8 per cent year-on-year, while sedans have stabilised at around 9 per cent share in FY2026. This is not a temporary dip; it is a sustained multi-year contraction. The segment that once defined Indian car ownership is being marginalised by affordable compact SUVs priced in the same 7 to 12 lakh range.This shift has triggered a clear industry response. Most OEMs are focusing heavily on SUVs, especially compact and sub-4-metre models, which sit in the sweet spot of affordability and aspiration. But the German automaker, Volkswagen is taking a slightly different route in India. Instead of abandoning declining segments like hatchbacks, the brand is revisiting them alongside its SUV push.Earlier this year, Volkswagen had announced its plans for product interventions in every quarter of the calendar year 2026 in India. This roadmap includes SUVs, sedans, and hatchbacks, signaling Volkswagen’s intent to expand beyond its current SUV-heavy portfolio. Tayron R-Line marked the first product intervention earlier this year, with the Taigun Facelift launching this month, while the company has already revealed the car.And this is where its 2026 strategy becomes interesting, balancing SUVs with renewed focus on hatchbacks and performance-led global models.Hatchbacks and Sub-4m SUV OpportunityVolkswagen’s history in India with hatchbacks, particularly the Polo, continues to shape its thinking. In a conversation, Nitin Kohli, Brand Director of Volkswagen India, acknowledged the shift in consumer preference but pointed hatchbacks remain an important part of Volkswagen’s global portfolio and could play a role in future India plans, especially with evolving free trade agreements (FTA) enabling faster model introductions.“If you look at the history of the brand and even last year when we introduced our hot hatch Golf GTI, it was sold out in two days. Whether it is the Polo or the Golf GTI, these interventions have done really well and the customers appreciate them. For us, it was, of course, a segment which really did well and there is a need for more,” Kohli said.He indirectly hinted at the strategic gap left behind by the Volkswagen Polo. Though, the reference to Polo and Golf GTI suggests that Volkswagen is closely monitoring demand for premium hatchbacks and performance-oriented compact cars and is not completely exiting the hatchback space. Instead, the company is evaluating opportunities, especially as global portfolio access improves through policy changes.With the India–EU Free Trade Agreement under discussion, the ability to bring global models as fully built imports and test market response could accelerate Volkswagen’s hatchback strategy. Kohli noted, “Today, with the ongoing discussions of FTA, while the fine print is not very clear, this gives us an opportunity to get the cars faster, test the waters, and then follow different paths to get to the volume side of it.”While Volkswagen has not officially confirmed the return of the Polo, the brand clearly sees value in the hatchback identity again and could bring it as a CBU first to test the response and later localise to achieve volumes. The Polo was launched in India in 2010 and went on to establish a strong presence in the premium hatchback segment over more than a decade. However, it was discontinued in 2022 due to low sales and changing trends. According to data from the Society of Indian Automobile Manufacturers (SIAM), Volkswagen sold only 11,816 units of the Polo in FY22. The low volumes, combined with evolving regulatory requirements and rising costs, weakened the business case for continuing the model.Piyush Arora, Managing Director & CEO, Škoda Auto Volkswagen India, resonated the tone, adding “We are exploring multiple segments and multiple product lines. The whole idea is that we first bring FBU (Fully Built Up) models, test the waters, then move to parts and components localisation, and then scale into India 2.0 products. Today, volumes are being driven by India 2.0 and our model interventions.”In addition to hatchbacks, the sub-4m SUV segment is another area under active evaluation. The segment accounts for a significant share of India’s passenger vehicle market and continues to attract intense competition due to its volume potential and tax advantages. However, Volkswagen is not rushing in.Kohli clarified the approach, stating “Sub-4m is a very volume-driven space. It caters to around 28–29 per cent of the segment in the A00 SUV, and even higher if you take the overall market. Our current focus is on India 2.0 cars and driving volume through them. We continue to evaluate different markets, segments and powertrains, and will introduce those vehicles at the right time.”This confirms Volkswagen is studying the segment but will only enter when it aligns with brand positioning and volume strategy. Rather than rushing into every high-volume segment, the company appears to be prioritising product differentiation, performance, and long-term sustainability under the Volkswagen Group’s India 2.0 strategyTaigun Driving Volkswagen’s GrowthWhile hatchbacks and sub-4m SUVs are future possibilities, the recently introduced Taigun facelift remains Volkswagen’s immediate volume driver. The company has sold over 1 lakh vehicles since 2020, with the Taigun alone contributing to 90 per cent of the volumes.Kohli explained “This is big for us; this is the volume game for us and the plan is to do higher volumes with this vehicle.”The segment itself has expanded dramatically in recent years, growing from just 5 key models about 5 years ago to nearly 20 today, intensifying competition and raising the bar for differentiation. Against this backdrop, Volkswagen expects the new Taigun to outperform typical facelift benchmarks.“Any model intervention that happens in the market typically sees sales growth of around 18 to 22 per cent. Our goal is to achieve higher than this with the new Taigun when it is launched in the market this month. This is the industry norm, and we should be beating this number,” Kohli said, emphasizing that the Taigun will play a central role in driving higher volumes for the brand.Powertrain Strategy: EVs and Transition TechUnlike many competitors who are aggressively diversifying in this segment with CNG, hybrids, and EV powertrain options, Volkswagen India remains strongly anchored in petrol-powered performance.Kohli explained that Volkswagen’s customer base continues to prioritise performance and driving dynamics over alternative powertrain options. “From the Volkswagen customer’s perspective, it is performance that drives the business for us. GT Plus and GT Line together contribute almost 60 per cent of our total model sales, and we focus heavily on this,” he said.However, the company is not ignoring the transition underway. “The group has already demonstrated leadership in EVs. It is easy for us to get those here but the policy has to also support us. It makes complete sense when EVs are locally manufactured, so it is not a matter of if, but when we introduce EVs in India,” Kohli added. Arora, reinforced this view, pointing out that regulatory clarity and market evolution will determine the pace of electrification in the country. He noted that current electric vehicle penetration, including hybrids, stands at around 4-4.5 per cent of the total industry, but is expected to grow in double digits by the end of the decade. “One thing is clear for our group as well that the future is electrification, but it will follow different trajectories in different geographies, and India will not be very different. By the end of this decade, this number should go into double digits, and that is the timeline we are looking at to enter this space,” Arora said.Also, on the industry-wide debate around hybrids vs EVs vs flex fuels, Volkswagen is keeping all options open but cautiously. Arora noted that the company is evaluating all options while emphasizing that electrification will coexist with transitional technologies such as mild hybrids, strong hybrids, plug-in hybrids, range extenders, and even ethanol blending solutions. According to Arora, “There are transitional needs which can be filled by different technologies, including hybrids and ethanol blending. But having said that, there is a need for clarity on that.” However, electrification remains the final destination, “We do believe that target or the future goal for industry or for at least Volkswagen Group is electrification, even though transitional technologies will continue to play a role.” Arora said.This suggests EVs will likely enter alongside future product interventions, supported by localisation and policy clarity.2026 Product RoadmapBeyond powertrains, Skoda Auto Volkswagen Group’s product strategy in India is becoming more aggressive and structured. The group is planning 18–19 product interventions across brands, while Volkswagen itself is committed to introducing at least one model every quarter. Arora confirmed, stating, “Overall, we are talking about 18–19 product intervention across the group… and this is what we are looking at for this year.”While Kohli emphasized, “The earlier announcement was that every quarter you will see one action from our side, and we remain committed to that. As of now, the plan stays firm that we will have four model interventions this year.”Volkswagen’s focus on customer experience is also shaping its strategy for the coming years. The brand currently operates around 274 touchpoints across India, covering nearly 85 per cent of the market, and plans to expand gradually in line with future product launches and volume growth. “The balance 15 per cent for us as a brand, maybe it is some time to reach that market but today I think we are adequately placed.  Last year, we had 14 new performance centers and we come with the outlets at the right time where we have the model interventions coming in,” Kohli notedExports as a Key Growth DriverVolkswagen Group’s export strategy is another critical pillar of its India operations. The group exports nearly 30 per cent of its total production from India, with volumes ranging between 40,000 and 50,000 units annually.Arora highlighted that Mexico remains a key export market for both Taigun and Virtus, although recent economic challenges and tariff-related developments have affected demand.“Mexico is a big market for Volkswagen, but we have seen the Mexican economy going down and demand softening. Also, Mexico is a big importer from China. And Chinese pricing capabilities are a little different than the rest of the automotive world. So, from that point of view, we are seeing some amount of impact on that. But we are working on that and are hopeful that that economy will revive as well.”While Skoda brand continues to explore new markets like Southeast Asia and Middle East, the group is also exploring new markets for VW brand, particularly in North Africa, including Egypt, as part of its growth strategy. Arora described this as a long-term expansion plan rather than a risk mitigation measure. “North Africa is something we are looking at more. Egypt can be one opportunity. It is a growth strategy because automotive markets are cyclic and expanding markets helps us balance those cycles,” he explained.Supply Chain & Geopolitical RisksGlobal geopolitical developments, including tensions in West Asia, are also being closely monitored by Volkswagen group. Arora acknowledged that while the conflict has not disrupted operations yet, however, risks remain. “The impact so far has not affected our operational business, but commodity prices have gone up and supply chain lead times may increase. If the conflict continues for a sustained period, cost implications may have to be passed on to customers,” he said, adding that lessons learned during the covid-19 period have strengthened supply chain resilience.“West Asia impact, we will see in the second quarter. Second quarter is a challenging quarter for automotive industry,” Arora added.Long-Term Strategy & OutlookIn a market driven by short-term trends, Volkswagen is playing a longer, more calculated game in India. With 1 product introduction every quarter, Taigun driving volumes, calculated EV strategy, FTA opportunities, and potential hatchback revival, Volkswagen appears to be preparing for its next phase in India. The biggest question now remains for enthusiasts, will the Polo return and will Volkswagen enter the sub-4 metre SUV space? The leadership is not committing yet but their statements suggest the doors are clearly open.

Earlier this year, Volkswagen had announced its plans for product interventions in every quarter of the calendar year 2026 in India. This roadmap includes SUVs, sedans, and hatchbacks, signaling Volkswagen’s intent to expand beyond its current SUV-heavy portfolio. Tayron R-Line marked the first product intervention earlier this year, with the Taigun Facelift launching this month, while the company has already revealed the car. And this is where its 2026 strategy becomes interesting, balancing SUVs with renewed focus on hatchbacks and performance-led global models. Hatchbacks and Sub-4m SUV Opportunity Volkswagen’s history in India with hatchbacks, particularly the Polo, continues to shape its thinking. In a conversation, Nitin Kohli, Brand Director of Volkswagen India , acknowledged the shift in consumer preference but pointed hatchbacks remain an important part of Volkswagen’s global portfolio and could play a role in future India plans, especially with evolving free trade agreements (FTA) enabling faster model introductions. “If you look at the history of the brand and even last year when we introduced our hot hatch Golf GTI, it was sold out in two days. Whether it is the Polo or the Golf GTI, these interventions have done really well and the customers appreciate them. For us, it was, of course, a segment which really did well and there is a need for more,” Kohli said. He indirectly hinted at the strategic gap left behind by the Volkswagen Polo. Though, the reference to Polo and Golf GTI suggests that Volkswagen is closely monitoring demand for premium hatchbacks and performance-oriented compact cars and is not completely exiting the hatchback space. Instead, the company is evaluating opportunities, especially as global portfolio access improves through policy changes. With the India–EU Free Trade Agreement under discussion, the ability to bring global models as fully built imports and test market response could accelerate Volkswagen’s hatchback strategy. Kohli noted, “Today, with the ongoing discussions of FTA, while the fine print is not very clear, this gives us an opportunity to get the cars faster, test the waters, and then follow different paths to get to the volume side of it.” While Volkswagen has not officially confirmed the return of the Polo, the brand clearly sees value in the hatchback identity again and could bring it as a CBU first to test the response and later localise to achieve volumes. The Polo was launched in India in 2010 and went on to establish a strong presence in the premium hatchback segment over more than a decade. However, it was discontinued in 2022 due to low sales and changing trends. According to data from the Society of Indian Automobile Manufacturers (SIAM), Volkswagen sold only 11,816 units of the Polo in FY22. The low volumes, combined with evolving regulatory requirements and rising costs, weakened the business case for continuing the model. Piyush Arora, Managing Director & CEO, Škoda Auto Volkswagen India , resonated the tone, adding “We are exploring multiple segments and multiple product lines. The whole idea is that we first bring FBU (Fully Built Up) models, test the waters, then move to parts and components localisation, and then scale into India 2.0 products. Today, volumes are being driven by India 2.0 and our model interventions.” In addition to hatchbacks, the sub-4m SUV segment is another area under active evaluation. The segment accounts for a significant share of India’s passenger vehicle market and continues to attract intense competition due to its volume potential and tax advantages. However, Volkswagen is not rushing in. Kohli clarified the approach, stating “Sub-4m is a very volume-driven space. It caters to around 28–29 per cent of the segment in the A00 SUV, and even higher if you take the overall market. Our current focus is on India 2.0 cars and driving volume through them. We continue to evaluate different markets, segments and powertrains, and will introduce those vehicles at the right time.” This confirms Volkswagen is studying the segment but will only enter when it aligns with brand positioning and volume strategy. Rather than rushing into every high-volume segment, the company appears to be prioritising product differentiation, performance, and long-term sustainability under the Volkswagen Group’s India 2.0 strategy Taigun Driving Volkswagen’s Growth While hatchbacks and sub-4m SUVs are future possibilities, the recently introduced Taigun facelift remains Volkswagen’s immediate volume driver. The company has sold over 1 lakh vehicles since 2020, with the Taigun alone contributing to 90 per cent of the volumes. Kohli explained “This is big for us; this is the volume game for us and the plan is to do higher volumes with this vehicle.” The segment itself has expanded dramatically in recent years, growing from just 5 key models about 5 years ago to nearly 20 today, intensifying competition and raising the bar for differentiation. Against this backdrop, Volkswagen expects the new Taigun to outperform typical facelift benchmarks. “Any model intervention that happens in the market typically sees sales growth of around 18 to 22 per cent. Our goal is to achieve higher than this with the new Taigun when it is launched in the market this month. This is the industry norm, and we should be beating this number,” Kohli said, emphasizing that the Taigun will play a central role in driving higher volumes for the brand. Powertrain Strategy: EVs and Transition Tech Unlike many competitors who are aggressively diversifying in this segment with CNG, hybrids, and EV powertrain options, Volkswagen India remains strongly anchored in petrol-powered performance. Kohli explained that Volkswagen’s customer base continues to prioritise performance and driving dynamics over alternative powertrain options. “From the Volkswagen customer’s perspective, it is performance that drives the business for us. GT Plus and GT Line together contribute almost 60 per cent of our total model sales, and we focus heavily on this,” he said. However, the company is not ignoring the transition underway. “The group has already demonstrated leadership in EVs. It is easy for us to get those here but the policy has to also support us. It makes complete sense when EVs are locally manufactured, so it is not a matter of if, but when we introduce EVs in India,” Kohli added. Arora, reinforced this view, pointing out that regulatory clarity and market evolution will determine the pace of electrification in the country. He noted that current electric vehicle penetration, including hybrids, stands at around 4-4.5 per cent of the total industry, but is expected to grow in double digits by the end of the decade. “One thing is clear for our group as well that the future is electrification, but it will follow different trajectories in different geographies, and India will not be very different. By the end of this decade, this number should go into double digits, and that is the timeline we are looking at to enter this space,” Arora said. Also, on the industry-wide debate around hybrids vs EVs vs flex fuels, Volkswagen is keeping all options open but cautiously. Arora noted that the company is evaluating all options while emphasizing that electrification will coexist with transitional technologies such as mild hybrids, strong hybrids, plug-in hybrids, range extenders, and even ethanol blending solutions. According to Arora, “There are transitional needs which can be filled by different technologies, including hybrids and ethanol blending. But having said that, there is a need for clarity on that.” However, electrification remains the final destination, “We do believe that target or the future goal for industry or for at least Volkswagen Group is electrification, even tho

research.title: Volkswagen Is Betting Big on India Again—Here's Why
